data.title: U.S. natural gas inventory net change of +75Bcf for week ended Sept 19
data.description:
• Stocks for the week ended September 19 were 3,508 Bcf vs. 3,433 Bcf for the week ended September 12.
  • Net change: +75Bcf vs. +90Bcf for the week ended September 12.
  • Consensus: +76B.
  • Futures (NG1:COM) +2.3%
  • ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (FCG), (UNL), (HNU:CA)
  • Click here to read the full EIA Weekly Natural Gas Storage Report.

data.title: Kodiak gains as Barclays upgrades on Roche data for eye disease drug
data.description:
Shares of Kodiak Sciences (NASDAQ:KOD) climbed on Thursday after Barclays upgraded the eye drug developer, citing a positive read-through from a recent mid-stage trial readout shared by Roche (OTCQX:RHHBY) for an experimental therapy targeting diabetic macular edema (DME).

Analyst Gena Wang argued that Kodiak’s DME candidate KSI-101 has demonstrated an efficacy level largely similar to Roche's (OTCQX:RHHBY) vamikibart + ranibizumab combination, for which the Swiss drugmaker posted Phase 2 data this week during its Pharma Day.

“If KSI-101 were approved, noninferiority to the standard of care would be sufficient to make it an attractive option, given its favorable safety profile,” Wang wrote, based on feedback from a professor of ophthalmology.

Despite citing a limited opportunity for Kodiak’s (NASDAQ:KOD) other experimental drugs, tarcocimab and KSI-501, the analyst indicated KSI-101’s potential to become a differentiated option against macular edema secondary to inflammation (MESI).

Wang upgraded KOD to Equal Weight from Underweight and raised its price target to $17 from $7 per share.

data.title: BlackBerry raises full-year revenue guidance to $519M–$541M while expanding QNX design wins and profitability
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: BlackBerry Limited (BB:CA) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO John Giamatteo reported, "Q2 was another strong quarter for BlackBerry with all 3 of our divisions beating the top end of guidance. The company revenue for the quarter was stronger than expected, growing 3% year-over-year to $129.6 million." He highlighted that BlackBerry achieved a positive GAAP net income for the second consecutive quarter at $13.3 million, and non-GAAP EPS was positive $0.04. Giamatteo emphasized QNX's outperformance: "QNX beat expectations for both revenue and adjusted EBITDA to achieve a Rule of 40 quarter. We delivered 15% year-over-year revenue growth and a 32% adjusted EBITDA margin for Q2. QNX revenue for the quarter was $63.1 million, primarily driven by strong royalties."
  • Giamatteo detailed strategic wins, including a "mid-8-figure design win in the Chinese market with a leading global Tier 1 supplier to power ADAS applications" and partnerships with BMW and Qualcomm for the Snapdragon Ride Pilot platform built on QNX. He also cited product advancements such as the release of QNX OS for Safety 8.0 and a win to deliver QNX Sound to a leading Chinese automaker.
  • The Secure Communications division exceeded the top end of guidance with revenue of $59.9 million, driven by reduced customer churn for UEM and upside in both ad hoc and SecuSUITE. ARR grew by $4 million to $213 million and the DBNRR improved to 93%. Notable deals included a five-year hosted secure voice services contract with a German government agency and a large renewal with the U.S. State Department for ad hoc critical events management.
  • CFO Tim Foote stated, "Operating leverage driven by the strong top line, combined with tight cost control, enabled us to deliver expanded profit margins. Total company adjusted gross margins expanded by 4 percentage points year-over-year to 75% and remained flat sequentially." Foote added, "Total company adjusted EBITDA grew a very strong 72% year-over-year to $25.9 million. Adjusted net income for Q2 was $24.2 million, and GAAP net income was $13.3 million. This is a $33 million turnaround in GAAP net income from the $19.7 million loss in the prior year."

Outlook

  • Foote announced, "We are increasing our full year revenue guidance by $3 million at the midpoint, while also narrowing the range to $256 million to $270 million" for QNX and raising full year adjusted EBITDA guidance by $11 million at the midpoint to between $64 million and $73 million.
  • Secure Communications full year revenue guidance was increased to $239 million to $247 million, and adjusted EBITDA is now expected between $38 million and $48 million.
  • For the total company, Foote stated, "For the full fiscal year 2026, we are raising the midpoint for total company revenue by $7 million and now expect it to be between $519 million and $541 million. And we're raising guidance for adjusted EBITDA at the midpoint by $12 million to be in the range of $82 million to $101 million." Non-GAAP EPS guidance was also raised to between $0.11 and $0.15 for the full year.

Financial Results

  • BlackBerry reported total company revenue of $129.6 million, adjusted EBITDA of $25.9 million, adjusted net income of $24.2 million, and GAAP net income of $13.3 million. QNX revenue reached $63.1 million, with an adjusted EBITDA of $20.5 million. Secure Communications revenue was $59.9 million, with an adjusted EBITDA of $9.7 million, and Licensing revenue came in at $6.6 million with adjusted EBITDA of $5.6 million. Cash and investments at quarter end were $363.5 million.
  • The company returned approximately $20 million to shareholders through share repurchases, bringing total shares removed by the program to 7.6 million.

Q&A

  • Luke Junk, Baird: Asked about operating leverage in QNX and the outlook for costs. Foote responded, "I see a lot of leverage in the QNX model. We're already at gross margins of 83%. And over time, we should see that improve, particularly as the mix of Royalty starts to increase...I still see leverage through the model."
  • Junk followed up on the China market and ADAS design wins. Giamatteo explained, "We're seeing...the market shift more towards safety-critical software and the need for a high-performance type of capability...that trend is a positive one for us, and it certainly enabled us to make some progress this particular quarter."
  • Paul Treiber, RBC: Inquired about the back-end loaded QNX outlook. Foote stated, "The revenue trend or pattern really for QNX for the last couple of years, it has been back-end loaded...the biggest kind of moving part from quarter-to-quarter is development seat licenses...driven really by start of programs, that design work, and that tends to be towards the back end of the year."
  • Treiber asked about auto OEM platform prioritization. Foote answered, "I won't go as far as to say back on track...programs are starting to come online...the challenges of developing software remain complex, and those have certainly not gone away."
  • Thomas Ingham, CIBC: Asked about QNX backlog and growth sustainability. Foote said, "Q1 was weaker, but Q2 has come back pretty well and we're actually ahead of plan for Q2...the pipeline of opportunities looks really solid."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral, with questions focused on cost leverage, sustainability of QNX growth, and the impact of macro trends. No pressing skepticism was evident, but there was attention to potential headwinds and growth drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone during prepared remarks, frequently referencing "strong" and "solid" results. In Q&A, the tone was slightly more cautious, especially when discussing macro uncertainties and the pace of automotive recovery, as Foote stated, "I won't go as far as to say back on track...the challenges of developing software remain complex, and those have certainly not gone away."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the current sentiment is more optimistic, with less caution in guidance language and a clearer commitment to growth targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • BlackBerry raised full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for both QNX and Secure Communications, compared to a more cautious stance in Q1 which cited macro uncertainty and elongated buying cycles.
  • Management tone shifted from a prudent optimism in Q1 to greater confidence, backed by positive profit and cash flow performance and explicit increases in guidance ranges.
  • Analyst focus remained on QNX operating leverage, market momentum, and backlog growth, but with less concern over immediate macro headwinds than in the prior quarter.
  • Key metric changes include higher revenue, stronger EBITDA growth, and positive GAAP net income following a loss in the prior year. The company also increased its share buyback activity.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing macro uncertainty, particularly in auto, but stated, "the level of uncertainty has decreased...we are pricing in less downside risk in today's guidance than previously."
  • Foote noted, "the tariff uncertainty has now become kind of really just a more certain tariff headwind, but the challenges of developing software remain complex, and those have certainly not gone away."
  • Analyst questions highlighted risks around the timing of design wins, seasonality, and the pace at which automotive programs move into production.

Final Takeaway

BlackBerry delivered another quarter of growth, expanded profit margins, and positive cash flow, prompting management to raise full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance. Strong QNX performance, new design wins, and momentum in Secure Communications support optimism for continued growth, while management remains mindful of ongoing macro and market risks as the company aims for sustained profitability and cash generation in fiscal 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Steelcase signals continued momentum as merger with HNI nears and corporate demand strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Steelcase Inc. (SCS) Q2 2026

Management View

  • Sara Armbruster, President and CEO, reported that "in the second quarter, we delivered strong financial results that built on our recent momentum. Our revenue and adjusted earnings grew over the prior year and exceeded our expectations, and they marked our highest quarterly results over the past 5 years." Armbruster highlighted 5% revenue growth, led by large corporate customers in the Americas, and 13% growth in the International segment, with notable strength in India. She emphasized that "our proposed transaction with HNI is expected to further our strategy of expanding our reach within markets" and that the combination is expected by the end of calendar year 2025.

  • Armbruster noted, "our win rates continue to remain strong, which we believe is evidence that we continue to lead the transformation of the workplace. We saw strong growth this quarter from both the financial services sector and from large technology customers."

  • David Sylvester, Senior VP & CFO, stated, "our second quarter revenue of $897 million was above the estimated range we provided in June due to stronger-than-expected orders from our large corporate customers and favorable shipment timing in our Americas segment." Sylvester also reported that adjusted earnings of $0.45 per share finished above the estimated range, benefiting from higher revenue and favorable gross margins, and that "our adjusted operating margin of 8.4% in the second quarter represented a 40 basis point improvement compared to the prior year."

Outlook

  • Steelcase did not provide forward-looking guidance due to the pending HNI combination. Sylvester said, "in light of our pending combination with HNI, we will not be providing forward-looking guidance at this time."

  • Armbruster reiterated excitement for the merger, expressing confidence in realizing "the significant benefits that our combined company is expected to bring to our shareholders, customers, dealers and employees."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for the quarter was $897 million, attributed by Sylvester to "stronger-than-expected orders from our large corporate customers and favorable shipment timing in our Americas segment."

  • Adjusted earnings were $0.45 per share, with favorable gross margins due to a favorable business mix and cost controls.

  • The International segment posted 13% revenue growth, with 8% on an organic basis. The Americas achieved an adjusted operating margin of 11%, while International adjusted operating results improved by $5 million compared to the prior year.

  • Cash and short-term investments increased by $32 million from Q1, primarily due to $100 million of adjusted EBITDA, partially offset by $28 million in accounts receivable, $18 million in capital expenditures, and a $12 million dividend payment. Total liquidity was $427 million, and total debt was $447 million at quarter end.

Q&A

  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets: Asked for a breakdown between volume and price in the Americas order growth. Sylvester responded that "the 8% order growth, I would say more of it was driven by volume than price. I don't think the price benefits were more than a couple of percent."

  • Gomes asked about additional price increases. Sylvester declined to comment on future pricing actions but noted, "we were able to offset the year-over-year inflation and tariff costs with incremental pricing benefits that were related to the actions we took earlier in the year."

  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group: Inquired about the nature of demand in the Americas and whether it was return-to-office driven. Armbruster answered, "many of these clients are rethinking their office space...really focused on outcomes, really asking questions about kind of what is the space designed to support, whether that's creativity or collaboration or connection." Sylvester added that "our project orders grew at a faster rate than our continuing business, supporting Sara's remarks that a lot of this is about changing and transforming the office."

  • Ramsey asked about profitability improvement in International, especially Asia Pacific. Sylvester replied, "both, I believe, improved versus prior year. We feel pretty good about where the Asia Pacific business is at the moment...they were profitable in the quarter, and we feel pretty good about where they are from a demand perspective."

  • Reuben Garner, Benchmark: Asked about pricing actions and margin recovery. Sylvester clarified that in Q2, "the benefits from our pricing actions offset the tariffs and incremental inflation again year-over-year," but that it would "probably take another couple of quarters before we fully catch up on a cumulative basis and begin to get margin on all of that inflation and tariff costs."

  • Garner also asked about order patterns in the Americas and early Q3 trends. Sylvester said, "it was spread evenly across the quarter...the first 3 weeks are roughly flat compared to the prior year."

  • Garner probed on office space mix changes. Armbruster observed, "we see more and more customers really rethinking like how the space is going to support their strategic goals...they need privacy, they need collaboration solutions. They need technology implemented in different ways."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with most questions focusing on demand mix, pricing recovery, and the sustainability of recent momentum. Questions were direct, seeking clarification rather than pressing for negative disclosures.

  • Management maintained a positive and confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A. Armbruster and Sylvester expressed confidence in business momentum, the success of cost controls, and the strategic importance of the HNI merger. Sylvester used phrases like "we feel pretty good about where they are from a demand perspective" and "we are confident that the combination will be a win for all of our stakeholders."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained upbeat but was more focused on merger integration and demonstrated greater confidence in the sustainability of large corporate demand.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q2, Steelcase reported higher revenue ($897 million) and adjusted earnings per share ($0.45) compared to Q1 ($779 million revenue, $0.20 adjusted EPS).

  • The company continued to highlight strong large corporate demand, particularly in the Americas, with notable growth from the financial services and technology sectors.

  • The International segment showed improved profitability and revenue momentum, especially in India and Asia Pacific, building on trends discussed in Q1.

  • Management did not provide forward guidance this quarter, in contrast to Q1, citing the pending merger with HNI.

  • Analyst focus shifted from concerns about education and government sector weakness in Q1 to the sustainability of project-driven corporate demand and merger strategy in Q2.

  • Management's confidence in transformation efforts remained strong, and the company provided more details on cost reductions and strategic alignment for the merger.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macro weakness in Germany and France continued to impact International segment results, with management "focusing on winning as much available business as possible" and aligning resources to "focus on the best opportunities."

  • Education sector demand declined, attributed to changes in federal funding policy.

  • Volatility remains in inflation and tariff costs, with management noting "it remains quite volatile week-to-week."

  • The pending merger with HNI introduces uncertainty, and management is not providing forward-looking guidance during the transition period.

Final Takeaway

Steelcase delivered its highest quarterly results in five years, fueled by strong demand from large corporate customers and improved international performance, especially in India and Asia Pacific. The company emphasized continued momentum in workplace transformation projects and robust cost controls. While education and certain international markets remain challenged, management is focused on strategic alignment ahead of the HNI merger, which is expected to close by the end of 2025 and is anticipated to bring significant benefits to stakeholders.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Breeze Airways adds international flights as it looks to disrupt the low-cost airline sector
data.description:
U.S. airline upstart Breeze Airways plans to fly internationally for the first time in 2026 as it continues its aggressive entry into the lowe-cost airline industry.  

The airline will begin a Saturday-only route between Norfolk, Virginia, and Cancun, Mexico, on January 10, followed by round trips between Charleston, South Carolina, and Cancun on January 17, also only on Saturdays.

Breeze Airways is a U.S.-based low-cost airline founded by aviation entrepreneur David Neeleman, known for previously helping to launch JetBlue (JBLU), WestJet, and Azul (OTC:AZULQ). Headquartered in Cottonwood Heights, Utah, Breeze began operations in May 2021 and focuses on providing efficient and affordable flights primarily between secondary airports, intentionally bypassing major hubs to reduce travel time and serve routes with little or no competition.

The company's goal is to make air travel more convenient and accessible by connecting underserved cities across the U.S. Currently, the airline offers over 150 nonstop and "BreezeThru" routes, allowing passengers to fly between smaller cities or less commonly served airports. Notably, Breeze operates a modern fleet consisting of Airbus A220-300 and Embraer E190/E195 aircraft, selected for their efficiency and versatility on midsize routes. 

Earlier in the year, Breeze Airways announced that it will be bringing accessible air service to five new cities on the West Coast. The new service includes flights from Burbank, California; Arcata, California; Redmond, Oregon; Eugene, Oregon; and Pasco/Tri-Cities, Washington.

Since its launch, Breeze Airways has become the largest airline in several of its key markets and continues to expand, serving more than 76 cities in 34 states. The major investors in Breeze Airways include BlackRock (BLK), Knighthead Capital Management, Peterson Ventures, Sandlot Partners, and Aries Capital Partners. 

Breeze Airways main competitors are Avelo Airlines, Allegiant Air (ALGT), Frontier Airlines (ULCC), and what's left of Spirit Airlines (OTC:SAVEQ).

data.title: Citigroup shifts ~1,000 tech jobs to India - report
data.description:
Citigroup (NYSE:C) has shifted about 1,000 technology-related jobs to India after cutting its workforce in China, people familiar with the matter told Bloomberg News.

The jobs were moved in phases to the Indian business support centers over the past few months, the people, who asked not to be identified because the information isn't public, reportedly said.

As of June, the banking giant was set to cut ~3,500 jobs at its two major technology centers in China as a part of a broader global restructuring effort. The cuts were expected to be completed by the fourth quarter this year.

Citi had previously announced a plan to reduce its global workforce by 20,000 by the end of 2026.

Earlier this week, a media report had said that Wall Street banks are poised to lean more on their Indian business support centers with the Trump administration hitting a $100K fee on new applications for the widely used H-1B visa program.

U.S. banks are reportedly among the biggest employers in the global capability centers, allowing them to shift work to India to avoid the pricier H-1B visas.

data.title: West scrambles to build Shahed-style drones as Russia’s costly salvos shift warfare
data.description:
The widespread use of Iran’s inexpensive, long-range Shahed drones by Russia in Ukraine has prompted the U.S. and allied nations to accelerate development of similar, mass-produced attack UAVs, The Wall Street Journal reported Thursday.

Russia’s invasion of Ukraine has underscored that unmanned systems can be both cheap and precise, upending a decades-old division between costly guided missiles and lower-cost area weapons.

Shahed-type loitering munitions, reportedly costing only tens of thousands of dollars each and able to fly many hundreds, by some estimates more than 1,000, miles, have been used in large waves to overwhelm air defenses. Russian forces often pair drone barrages with missile strikes and decoys, increasing the odds that at least some weapons penetrate defensive layers.

Western militaries and companies from the United States to Europe and Asia are racing to field analogous systems. Officials and industry leaders say the lesson from Ukraine is stark: in a future high-intensity fight, large inventories of low-cost strike systems matter. As Lt. Gen. André Steur of the Royal Netherlands Air and Space Force put it, sustained conflict requires deep stockpiles and affordable options, the Journal reported.

Dozens of U.S. prototypes

U.S. defense contractors are testing dozens of prototypes; small firms such as SpektreWorks and Griffon Aerospace have unveiled designs that mimic the Shahed’s triangular planform and mass-production simplicity, while larger suppliers are pitching faster, more expensive alternatives they say justify higher unit costs through improved survivability and effectiveness. For example, British firm MGI says its SkyShark is far quicker and therefore harder to intercept, arguing that higher hit rates can make the pricier drone more economical in practice.

Cost remains a central challenge. Western drones frequently carry premium price tags, often into the hundreds of thousands or millions once training and support are included. In contrast, Russia’s Shahed variants are believed to be produced for a fraction of that amount. Analysts note the Shahed’s simple construction of triangular wings, propeller propulsion and basic structural materials helps keep per-unit costs very low.

Manufacturers are also marketing realistic target drones that emulate Shahed performance so air-defense units can train against the threat. At the same time, some firms are blending cruise-missile range and UAV flexibility: MBDA says its new salvo-fired attack drone combines elements of a cruise missile with an unmanned platform designed for mass launch and attrition of defenses.

Experts warn that the proliferation of cheap, long-range loitering munitions changes force planning and strategic balances. James Patton Rogers of Cornell’s Brooks Tech Policy Institute says massed, low-cost precision strikes that saturate defenses are among the most serious new security risks, and that Western forces need a broader mix of electronic, physical and kinetic countermeasures as well as cheaper strike options of their own, the Journal reported.

data.title: Wall Street drops as jobless claims fall and Q2 GDP was revised higher
data.description:
Wall Street’s recent downward push continued on Thursday as the broader market averages observe selling pressure after jobless claims fell and GDP was revised higher in the third estimate.

Falling the hardest has been the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) which has dropped 0.8%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has found itself lower by 0.5% and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) is in the red by 0.1%. 

Across sectors, seven of the 11 S&P groups are in negative trading territory, with Communication Services suffering the most. At the other end, Energy has been the strongest performing segment on the session so far. 

U.S. Treasury yields are also on the move higher. The longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) pushed up 3 basis points to 4.18% and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up 5 basis points to 3.65%. At the same time the dollar index (DXY) tagged a 3-week high.

On the economic front, initial jobless claims dropped for the second week.

Q2 GDP was revised higher to +3.8% in the third estimate, intensifying the rebound.

Durable goods orders surprised higher in August, while the U.S. international goods trade deficit narrowed more than expected in August.

“Thursday's upward GDP revision for second quarter confirmed that the economy grew at a healthy clip, even as tariff uncertainty reached fever pitch during the quarter. The U.S. economy is resilient and the strong GDP is another indication that we are not at risk of any kind of recession, even with slowing labor market growth,” Paul Stanley, chief investment officer, Granite Bay Wealth Management stated.

“Thursday's GDP strength likely doesn't change the Federal Reserve's expected path of rate cuts, since the data is backward looking and was already well-known. The Fed wants to lower rates to keep the labor market in check and is able to do so at this time since inflation has remained under control, even though it's still above the Fed's target,” Stanley added.

Furthermore, the Fed balance sheet will come in later in the day.

In terms of Fed speak, Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee, New York Fed President John Williams, Fed Governor Michael Barr and San Francisco Fed President Mary Daly, among others, are all slated to take the stage in different events.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan extends slide as Grasberg accident sparks concerns over global supplies
data.description:
Freeport-McMoRan (NYSE:FCX) shares extend losses in early trading Thursday, -5.2% to a five-month low, after the top copper miner declared force majeure on contracted supplies from its Grasberg mine in Indonesia following a fatal mudslide accident.

Freeport (NYSE:FCX) was the S&P 500's worst performer in the previous session, sinking nearly 17% in its largest single-day decline since March 18, 2020, according to data from Dow Jones.

Comex copper (HG1:COM) for September delivery -0.9% to $4.716/lb in early trading after surging 3.6% on Wednesday.

Analysts say a prolonged disruption at Grasberg - the world’s second-largest copper mine, accounting for ~3% of global production - could drive copper prices even higher, while intensifying supply challenges for smelters already facing feedstock shortages.

"This latest event highlights the copper market’s sensitivity to supply disruptions, [and] raises concerns about future growth in supply," ANZ analysts said in a note.

The percentage of copper production hit by unplanned disruptions has increased steadily from just under 5% in 2014 to 5.7% in 2024, and is expected to surpass 6% this year, and "over the longer term, this will constrain supply growth, creating ongoing tightness in the copper market," ANZ said.

Goldman Sachs cut its global copper mine supply forecast for 2025 and 2026, now estimating a total loss of 525,000 metric tons of copper mine supply as a result of the Grasberg disruption, lowering its global mine supply forecast by 160,000 tons for H2 2025 and by 200,000 tons for full-year 2026, and seeing Grasberg's production declining by 250,000-260,000 tons in 2025 and by 270,000 tons in 2026.

The revisions pushed Goldman's 2025 global copper balance from a small surplus of 105,000 tons into a small deficit of 55,000 tons.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:CPER), (NYSEARCA:COPX), (OTC:JJCTF)

data.title: Jabil falls despite Q4 revenue boosted by AI demand; auto sector potentially impacted by 'regulatory shifts'
data.description:
Shares of Jabil (NYSE:JBL) fell about 10% on Thursday despite fiscal fourth quarter results and outlook surpassing estimates.

The electronics manufacturing services provider's non-GAAP EPS jumped about 43% year-over-year to $3.29 versus consensus of $2.95. 

“Strength in AI-driven demand across capital equipment, data centers, and networking, combined with deliberate portfolio actions in Connected Living & Digital Commerce, more than offset pressures in Automotive and Renewables, highlighting the resilience of our diversified business model," said JBL's CEO Mike Dastoor.

For the fourth quarter ended Aug. 31, revenue grew around 18.5% year-over-year to $8.25B, compared to the consensus estimate of $7.67B.

About the Automotive and Transport sector, Steve Borges, executive vice president, Regulated Industries, at Jabil, said on the earnings call that, "this industry is emerging from a global market correction, while near-term growth of battery electric vehicles is slowed, long term outlook remains strong."

Borges added that "regulatory shifts, changing incentives, and trade pressures, particularly around Chinese EVs and U.S. tariffs, are reshaping OEM strategies."

Matt Crowley, executive vice president, Intelligent Infrastructure at Jabil, said that "what differentiates Jabil, is that we are positioned across the entire AI hardware ecosystem. Not just a single piece of the puzzle but the combination of compute, storage, networking, power, cooling, and the tools that make all of this possible." 

"Our strategy is to engage at the rack scale level. Instead of supplying individual parts, we are integrating full racks into one deliverable system, Crowley added.

Outlook

Jabil expects fiscal first quarter net revenue to be between $7.7B and $8.3B (midpoint at $8B) versus consensus of $7.50B.

“Jabil is well positioned to deliver sustainable value creation over the long term, with significant opportunities ahead in areas such as AI data center infrastructure, healthcare, and advanced warehouse and retail automation. We remain focused on deploying capital in ways that strengthen our capabilities and enhance shareholder returns,” Dastoor noted.

The company expects non-GAAP core diluted EPS to be in the range of $2.47 to $2.87 (midpoint at $2.67, versus consensus of $2.40.

For the fiscal year 2026, Jabil expects net revenue to be about $31.3B compared to consensus of $30.98B.

The company noted on the call that for the first quarter, Regulated Industries revenue is expected to be $3.05B, Intelligent Infrastructure revenue is anticipated to be $3.67B driven by AI-related growth.

Revenue from Connected Living & Digital Commerce revenue is expected to be $1.29B.

The company anticipates non-GAAP core EPS of about $11 versus consensus of $10.84.

Update: The story was updated with executive commentary from the company's earnings call.
